summary: This post documents Q's experience being stopped by police in the Bronx while driving a new car—the fourth time that week. After refusing to show his license due to the officer's aggressive tone and being subjected to racial slurs, Q was arrested on shifting charges (tinted windows, then a warrant for littering, then disorderly conduct). Police deleted his recordings of the incident, lost his possessions until a white friend arrived, and disabled his car. The piece illustrates how routine harassment and systemic abuse target Black men, with Q noting this happens 'every single day.'
key_points: Q was stopped four times in one week for 'driving while Black,' with police using pretextual excuses like tinted windows
The charges against Q shifted three times (tinted windows, littering warrant, disorderly conduct), revealing the arbitrary nature of the arrest
Police deleted Q's recordings of the incident, destroying evidence of their misconduct
Q's possessions were 'lost' until a white, well-dressed friend arrived—whom police assumed was a lawyer
The experience reflects a daily reality for Black men, where clothing and appearance determine whether you're treated as a citizen or criminal
money_quotes: The officers told Q to 'give me your fucking license'
He recorded them saying, 'We should just lock this black guy up.'
Q later found out his recordings had been deleted.
She said there was no warrant on his record and that he'd been locked up for disorderly conduct. This was the third charge he'd heard about.
Q thinks they assumed his friend was a lawyer because he was white and well dressed.
If you're wearing a suit and tie it's different, but when you're wearing something fitted and some jeans, you're treated as a criminal.
It's sad that people have to go through this on a daily basis, for what?
key_insight: The criminal justice system operates with such arbitrary power over Black men that charges can shift three times, evidence can be deleted, and property can vanish—until a white witness appears.
